data.title: MKOR: Decent Credentials And Potential To Recover
data.description:
Picking Up The Baton From MAKOX At More Competitive Costs

The landscape of actively managed “ETFs” with a geographic focus on Korea isn’t littered with a plethora of options; the Matthews Korea Active ETF (NYSEARCA:MKOR), with less than $50m in total AUM, is currently one of the handful of geographically themed active ETFs that make up this developing terrain. As the name suggests, MKOR offers active coverage to a narrow pool of stocks based in South Korea (both common and preferred). However, what’s interesting to note is that while MKOR only got listed as an ETF in July 2023, it is essentially carrying the baton from the now defunct Matthews Korea Fund (MAKOX), which had been around from October 2010 until July 2023. MKOR has since picked up the assets and liabilities of MAKOX, and continues to operate under similar investing principles, albeit at a much more competitive cost for investors (the expense ratio of the fund was 1.13%, but MKOR’s expense ratio is priced a lot lower at just 0.79%). So, what does that constitute?

Well, they seek to leverage fundamental bottoms-up analysis encompassing the strength of the balance sheet, cash flow sustainability, general financial health, quality of the management, product lines, corporate governance, marketing strategies, etc. that eventually lead to Korean stocks capable of sustainable growth over time.

Was the active management particularly helpful before conversion to the ETF structure? Well, if we measure it against the most popular Korean product - the iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) - you’ll see that its average annual returns per year (5.4%) before conversion were superior to the latter (4.3%). Having said that, we also recognize that some investors may be perturbed by the degree of losses in a couple of bad years (2018 and 2022).

Matthews Korea In Its New Avatar vs iShares MSCI Korea ETF

Since 2023, MKOR has been run by two lead managers - Elli Lee and Sojung Park - who have honed their stock picking skills for roughly 8-9 years at Matthews Asia. They are also ably supported by Michael Oh, who served as co-manager, and appears to be a lot more entrenched in this business, having been around with Matthews Asia for over 25 years! So far, in the ETF cloak, their efforts appear to be paying off; Korean stocks in general have been on a slump since the second half of 2021 but MKOR is still outperforming EWY by over 100bps since it got listed as an ETF.

Crucially, these fund managers are also optimizing the portfolio well to take on lower risks; note that the rolling volatility profile has been consistently lower than EWY’s (despite covering a much smaller pool of stocks) with the differential now working out to over 550bps!

One of the key differentiators here is that EWY appears to tilt very heavily towards the giant and large-caps (92% of the portfolio), with limited exposure to mid-caps and no exposure to small and micro-caps either. Note also that the average market-cap of EWY’s portfolio is over 2x higher than our focus product. While MKOR too has significant exposure to giant and large-caps (two-thirds of the portfolio), it also brings a healthy dose of small and micro-cap coverage, while mid-cap exposure is also quite respectable at 20%. A lot of these giant and large-cap stocks are more heavily oriented towards the forces of global markets, whereas Korea small and micro-caps are a better proxy of domestic conditions.

When it comes to other structural qualities, note that both products are quite top-heavy (the top 10 stocks account for over 50% of the total portfolio in both cases), but given that EWY covers a much wider pool of stocks (almost 3x more than MKOR) we thought it ought to have a much lower top 10 weight. What this suggests is that there may be quite a few stocks in EWY that are just making up the numbers and MKOR is more efficient with its stock picking.

Given that MKOR is an actively managed product, there’s no surprise to discover that it costs more than EWY (an expense ratio differential of 20bps), and also resorts to more churn (1.7x the level of annual churn that EWY witnesses). However, it more than makes up for this by a much superior yield equating to almost 5% (more than twice as high as EWY’s current yield of 2.26%).

Is MKOR A Good Buy Now?

As implied in the section above, given how difficult it has been for Korean equities by and large, MKOR has fared relatively well, while also offering a very tasty yield. Investors may also be interested to note that MKOR’s portfolio of stocks can be picked up at very compelling valuations relative to the globe. MKOR is priced at less than 10x P/E, which represents almost a 50% discount to the corresponding multiple of global stocks, as represented by the Vanguard Total World Stock ETF. Crucially, at such a steep P/E discount, MKOR’s holdings are also poised to deliver slightly superior long-term earnings growth of 11%.

In light of this mismatch, we feel that Korean stocks represent an ideal mean reversion bet for those looking at beaten down opportunities across the globe; as you can see from the image below, MKOR’s relative strength ratio versus the globe is around 19% off its mean

Having said all that, anyone contemplating a position in Korea now should keep in mind that trade-related commentary is likely to prompt plenty of volatility in these stocks.

Take a look at MKOR’s weekly developments since the end of last year. Broadly we can see that MKOR had been forming a trading range between the $22-$27 levels, but amidst some strong bearish pressure which kick-started in September (marked by a downward sloping red trendline), the ETF broke support in December, and likely looked on course for a long slump. Yet in recent weeks we’ve seen some good firefighting skills at the lower levels, with MKOR not only negating the trendline, but also recouping its old trading range. As things stand, within this trading range, the risk-reward for a long position looks favorable.

Coming back to tariffs, what’s a little heartening to note is that compared to most other trading partners of the US who remain vulnerable to reciprocal tariffs, South Korea is better positioned (even though it levies the second-highest tariff rates after India) as a free trade pact has obliterated the effect of this with only limited tariffs on rice. However, what could be particularly concerning is if the Trump administration starts playing around with non-tariff barriers such as VAT, taxes on digital services, etc. which may end up hurting South Korea.

Domestically, South Korea looks set to embark on a more fruitful journey, after a difficult period (GDP growth in Q4-24 only saw a 0.1% sequential increase) supported by the Bank of Korea’s (BoK) impetus to ease financing conditions. After holding rates steady in January, the market is budgeting for a 25bps cut this month. Meanwhile, consumer confidence is certainly not as resilient as before, but it has been moving higher for the last couple of months.

Similar improvements can also be seen with business confidence, which had been on a slump for six straight months, before posting an improvement this month.

All in all, we feel that despite the prospect of high volatility, the cheaply priced, and high-yielding MKOR may be worth a shot now.

data.title: L3Harris Technologies: Back In The Buy Zone Amid DOGE Woes (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I began accumulating shares of defense contractor L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX) in early 2023 when the market turned increasingly cautious about the rather bold acquisition of Aerojet Rocketdyne, especially given the comparatively high implied leverage and significant supply chain issues at the time. As the latter began to resolve and L3Harris management commented favorably on the Aerojet Rocketdyne integration in its subsequent earnings reports, LHX shares began to recover significantly - somewhat too quickly in my opinion.

In my last article, published in July 2024, I tempered my optimism for LHX stock in light of what, I thought, was quite a bit of advance praise. Since then, and especially after the election of Donald Trump and subsequent statements of intent from him and Elon Musk related to a significant cut in defense spending, L3Harris stock has let off quite a bit of steam. To date, LHX shares are down more than 25% from their - somewhat lofty - 52-week high.

With this in mind, I think it's time to re-evaluate the L3Harris Technologies investment case. In this update, I would like to present a fresh view on LHX stock, taking into account the company's recently released full-year 2024 results. Based on the updated fundamentals and management's outlook for 2025 and beyond, I will also provide a valuation update and conclude by explaining why I expect to add more LHX shares to my existing position.

L3Harris Technologies: Solid 2024 Results, And Focusing On What Matters Most

For 2024, L3Harris delivered adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $13.10, up 6% year-over-year and almost perfectly in line with estimates. Total revenues of $21.3 billion were also in line with estimates, up 10% year-over-year (+4% organically). Services revenues grew in line with total revenue, which is very encouraging (Figure 1, red). For 2025, management expects revenues of $22.0 billion (+/- $200 million), which translates to a rather subdued growth expectation of 3.2%. On slide 3 of the earnings presentation, management stated that they intend to achieve average annual organic revenue growth of 4% between 2023 and 2026, which implies growth of approximately 4.5% for 2026.

On a GAAP basis, earnings per share were significantly lower at only $7.87, which warrants a brief assessment of whether management's adjustments were appropriate.

The largest adjustment - 34% of non-GAAP EPS - resulted from the amortization of acquisition-related intangible assets and inventory value adjustments (see p. 17 and p. 19 of the earnings press release). Another $1.4 per share was due to the implementation of "LHX NeXt", the company's restructuring program (see this article for more details). While I have no reservations about the former adjustment, I am keeping an eye on potentially long-term recurring restructuring costs and watching how well the company meets the associated financial commitments.

Similarly, I would like to see the costs associated with mergers and acquisitions and divestitures gradually decline. Also, I believe L3Harris should stop acquiring companies and instead focus on more focused and increasingly profitable activities. M&A and divestiture-related losses and expenses represented a $0.83 per share headwind to 2024 EPS.

The results for 2024 show first signs of improvement, but it will of course still take some time before L3Harris can achieve an operating margin in the high teens. An adjusted margin of 15.4% was reported for 2024, 60 bps higher than in the previous year. Reported selling, general and administrative (SG&A) expenses decreased by 33 bps year-over-year to 16.7% of total revenues. In 2023, SG&A costs fell by 51 bps compared to 2022. In my opinion, these improvements are the result of good cost control, particularly in light of the ongoing restructuring.

Gross profitability weakened in 2024, at least on a reportable basis (-43 bps), but I found the significant recovery in the Services segment gross margin encouraging (Figure 2, red bars). However, I think that a return of the Services gross margin to the high 20% range is a critical aspect that management needs to pay a lot of attention to. Not only are service revenues more predictable and less cyclical than product revenues, but I also believe that this is where the majority of cash flows should ultimately be generated.

Looking at L3Harris' four reporting segments, the 8% year-over-year revenue growth in the Communications Systems segment is particularly noteworthy, as is the 50 bps improvement in the adjusted operating margin. In this context, it is worth noting that L3Harris is benefiting from increased demand from NATO member states in this segment, which should provide a continued tailwind. I do not currently attach too much weight to the possible withdrawal of the U.S. from NATO and the associated secondary effects under President Trump's second term in office - in either direction. Western Europe in particular will step up a gear in terms of defense spending, and here I see L3Harris well positioned, as well as larger defense companies from both the U.S. and Europe. At the same time, one could argue that the expected massive increase in the bloc's defense spending will need to be funded somehow, and this would likely result in a further weakening of the EUR against the USD - in my view mainly due to the expected funding via quantitative easing measures.

While the other segments - with the exception of Aerojet Rocketdyne - showed more muted revenue growth in 2024 (Figure 3), the 40bps (Space & Airborne Systems), 100bps (Integrated Mission Systems), and 90bps (Aerojet Rocketdyne) expansion in adjusted operating margin confirmed the progress in efficiency gains, although I would reiterate that the company still has a lot of work to do before I would consider profitability to be adequate.

Free cash flow in 2024 was fairly strong at around $2.3 billion on a reported basis, consistent with my own calculation, which includes working capital adjustments and the treatment of stock-based compensation as a cash expense. For 2025 and 2026, management projected $2.45 billion and $2.8 billion, respectively (Figure 4), however, excluding the impact of stock-based compensation (around 4%-5% of free cash flow). Note that Figure 4 also shows L3Harris' adjusted free cash flow margin (red line), which has returned to 2020-2021 levels in 2024. Taking into account management's guidance, free cash flow profitability is expected to be flat in the coming years (red line), which once again underlines that the company is in the midst of a multi-year restructuring program.

Given the solid - and growing - backlog (+4.6% year-over-year, Figure 5) and increasingly meaningful revenue visibility, I would argue that L3Harris management's free cash flow guidance can be trusted. Therefore, I will incorporate L3Harris' current adjusted free cash flow and management's guidance in the updated valuation discussed in the last part of the article.

Considering that L3Harris' 2024 10-K was filed last week, on February 14, it's a good time to take a fresh look at the company's financial stability as well. Long-term debt accounts for more than 90% of the company's total debt, almost all of which is fixed-rate debt. This shields L3Harris from changes in short-term interest rates (which typically form the basis for floating-rate debt coupon rates), but the company's ability to service its debt could still weaken in the event of refinancing transactions. Between 2025 and 2033, $8.4 billion will mature, spread relatively evenly over the years:

Refinancing a portion of the bonds at maturity would result in a decrease in L3Harris' interest coverage ratio, but not really much as the coupon of the maturing debt is already in the mid to high 4% range. Considering that the company generates annual free cash flow of around $800 million to $1.0 billion after dividends and share buybacks (averaging $500 million per year), and amid management's pledge to further reduce leverage from the current 2.9x net debt to adjusted EBITDA (slide 14 of the earnings presentation), I would argue that leverage is under control. In light of the gradually improving leverage following the acquisitions, I was not too surprised (but nevertheless pleased) that the rating agency Moody's affirmed L3Harris' Baa2 rating and changed the rating outlook from negative to stable.

Management's continued focus on achieving profitability targets and paying down debt (as well as a current increased emphasis on share buybacks) does not bode well for the dividend growth part of the LHX investment case, at least in theory and judging by recent history (Figure 7).

I want to make it clear that for me as a long-term investor, financial stability is a higher priority than high dividend growth, so I can overlook another year or two of low single-digit dividend increases. However, given management's free cash flow and payout ratio guidance for 2025 and 2026, I wouldn't rule out a somewhat more significant dividend increase later this week or early next week. After all, paying out 40% of free cash flow in 2025 and 2026 would represent an increase of 10% and 14%, respectively, based on the upper end of the guidance. During the earnings call, the company management emphasized its intention to "maintain a competitive dividend" and buy back shares worth at least $1 billion in 2025. Hopefully, share buybacks are reduced somewhat going into 2026, as this would leave sufficient cash flow on the table to further reduce debt.

Conclusion

Defense contractor L3Harris Technologies Inc. has delivered a solid 2024 earnings report that shows early signs that the company's restructuring program is taking hold. At the same time, I would argue that the company still has a lot of work to do, considering the significant acquisitions and also the still apparent overhang from the merger between Harris Corporation and L3 Technologies in mid-2019 and the pandemic lockdowns-related secondary effects.

The progress is illustrated, for example, by the $800 million in gross cost savings realized by 2024, which is twice as high as originally expected. While the significant cost reductions in a short period of time are indeed a sign that much can still be improved at L3Harris, I would argue that management is on the right track, especially in light of the $400 million of additional savings planned for 2025 ($200 million more than originally expected).

As a result of the gradual improvement in profitability, but also the recovery from the severe supply chain disruptions since the pandemic, free cash flow is expected to increase significantly in the coming years. The order backlog remains high, at around 1.6 times 2024 revenues at year-end, and the consolidation of Aerojet Rocketdyne gives management a much better view of longer-term revenues and therefore free cash flow.

All in all, I am positive that L3Harris can generate free cash flow of $2.4 billion to $2.7 billion in the coming years. Against this backdrop, I am increasingly confident that the company can reduce leverage while continuing to pay a (modestly) growing dividend and buy back around $500 million worth of shares each year. For 2025, twice this amount is expected, representing roughly 2.7% of the current market capitalization. Personally, I expect that dividend growth will remain in the low single digits for a few more years, given the still quite improvable profitability and the currently elevated leverage of 2.9x net debt to adjusted EBITDA. However, given the dividend growth guidance of up to 10% as implied by the expected free cash flow payout ratio, I look forward to next week with a little excitement, although I wouldn't rule out another $0.02 quarterly increase.

Factoring the free cash flow estimates - as well as pretty modest long-term expectations - into the discounted cash flow valuation model for LHX stock (Figure 8), I feel confident about adding more shares to the position I started building in early 2023. Based on a cost of equity of 8.5%, LHX shares would be worth $235 today, which, I believe, is a reasonable margin of safety. The FAST Graphs chart in Figure 9 also points to an increasingly compelling valuation, which currently stands at a blended price-to-earnings ratio of 15. I don't expect any significant headwinds from DOGE-related cost savings, as I believe these will be largely focused on overhead and not really on the actual defense products and related services. In fact, I wouldn't rule out the possibility that contractors like L3Harris might even benefit despite a modest cut in the defense budget due to materially reduced overhead.

Nonetheless, due to the comparatively weaker bargaining position with the Department of Defense, I intend to keep my L3Harris position smaller than my other defense holdings Lockheed Martin Corp. (LMT), RTX Corp. (RTX), Northrop Grumman Corp. (NOC) and more recently also General Dynamics Corp. (GD), which I expect to cover in an upcoming article.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Alibaba: The Party Has Started, But You're Not Late Yet
data.description:
For as long as I have been writing articles on Seeking Alpha, I have always expressed a strong buy rating for Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA). In my last article dated December 2024, I highlighted several growth catalysts for 2025, and indeed what I predicted is happening:

  • Aid from the Chinese government is fueling consumption, and the economy seems more dynamic and ready to rebound. Just as the government has been a hiccup in previous years, today it will play the opposite role.
  • Cloud business is finally growing in double digits, and demand for artificial intelligence services is higher than ever.
  • People are gradually realizing that Alibaba is a tech giant that dominates in the East, just as companies like Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) and Meta (META) dominate in the West: it cannot be traded at a ridiculous PE Ratio. Moreover, the price per share is fueled by continued buybacks.

December Quarter 2024 results, BABA's fiscal Q3, have just come out, and in my opinion, shareholders could not have hoped for better. There has been growth on all fronts, and the future looks much brighter.

Highlights of December Quarter 2024

Alibaba's main problem was a difficult macroeconomic environment in China, stemming mainly from the bursting of the housing bubble. So many Chinese families found themselves without a home despite having paid for it, and obviously this situation had a chain reaction on consumption in the whole country. Much of Alibaba's revenue depends on e-commerce, mainly through Taobao and Tmall, and as a result, these platforms have stalled in revenue growth. The result has been widespread pessimism toward Alibaba, to the point of considering it a company with no room for growth.

However, the Chinese government has intervened to support the economy over the past year and may continue to do so in the coming months. These stimuli obviously do not have an immediate effect on the economy, something the market has been expecting given its impatience. The beneficial effect on the Chinese economy is only now becoming visible, and Tmall and Taobao are finally returning to growth.

Revenues are up 5% from December Quarter 2023, and adjusted EBITA has improved by 2%. This may sound like a modest result, but it is not at all. The market was discounting that this business was destined for an inexorable decline, which is no longer the case. In my opinion, the Chinese economy is restarting, and the fiscal stimulus is probably not over.

So, there is a lot of room for growth in China after years of stalling, meanwhile internationally, Alibaba has never stopped growing.

International Commerce revenues are up 32% from December Quarter 2023, but losses continue to rise. Adjusted EBITA reports increasing losses, but it is not something to worry about. AliExpress is expanding worldwide and now profitability is not what management is looking for. At this stage, it is more important to gain more market share and Alibaba is succeeding.

Now let's turn to the Cloud, one of the factors that makes me most optimistic about the future of this company.

There has been a lot of criticism about this segment recently because its growth rate could not even break single digits. In the most critical phases, growth was virtually absent, and the market underestimated its Cloud, although it continues to have a dominant market share in China and more generally in the East.

Compared to December Quarter 2023, the growth was 13%, and honestly, it exceeded my expectations. Last quarter it had been 7%; I expected double digits in this quarter, but not 13%. Since the Cloud is a very scalable business, Adjusted EBITA grew 33%, much faster than revenues. Restarting this business is critical for Alibaba because it is potentially the one that can give a boost to profitability, and therefore to EPS. The main driver that led to this marked improvement was the growing adoption of AI- related products.

For some reason, the market believed that China was asleep and that the AI technology revolution was just a Western affair. After DeepSeek, this perception is changing and investors are realizing that they have underestimated China in this respect. Alibaba's Qwen model family is top-notch, and for the 6th quarter in a row, revenues related to artificial intelligence products increased in triple digits. I repeat, it is the 6th quarter in a row, not the first, but it took DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) to give the West a reality check.

As for the other businesses, they are all up, except Cainiao.

This was probably the only downside of this quarterly because Cainiao has the potential to be able to grow in double digits, but it has been a few quarters since its growth stalled. I expected a slight recovery for the world's largest e-commerce cross-border logistics company, but there was none. Management attributed this result to a series of restructurings regarding their e-commerce business.

Overall, Alibaba's revenues increased 8% YOY and reached $38.40 billion, while Adjusted EBITA increased 4% and was $4.50 billion. From a cash flow perspective, there was a marked improvement over last year.

Cash Flow from Operations reached RMB 70.91 billion, up 9.58% from December Quarter 2023. Free cash flow has decreased, but not because the company is weakening, on the contrary. It is investing heavily to improve the Cloud infrastructure, the performance of AI-related products as well as improving the efficiency of its e-commerce, domestic and international. The December Quarter 2024 CapEx was RMB 31.36 billion, 3.3x that of 2023.

So not only has Alibaba returned to near double-digit growth, but it is also investing like never before to take advantage of a new growth cycle. This is what a company with a long-term view does, which is why Alibaba remains firmly within my portfolio despite the sharp rise in price per share recently. Among other things, the financial position remains extremely solid.

Net cash is $51.85 billion, a massive figure considering how much the company is investing and how many shares it is buying back.

Shares worth $1.30 billion were purchased in the December Quarter 2024, a high value, although declining from $4.10 billion in the previous quarter.

Conclusion

The December 2024 quarter was a success from multiple perspectives:

  • The Cloud has returned to growth, and demand for AI-related products has been extremely high for the 6th quarter in a row. In addition, the profitability of this segment continues to improve and investment is increasing.
  • Taobao and Tmall are two giants that are rising from their ashes. The market used to give them up for dead, but the Chinese government's stimulus is having its first effects and more optimism is leaking out among consumers.
  • The financial position remains extremely solid.

Although Alibaba's price per share has risen considerably in the past month, I still consider this company a strong buy. The reasons are the following:

  • Alibaba's Cloud dominates in China and the East and is still in an early stage of growth. The potential is far greater than 13% growth, and I believe 2025 will be the year of redemption.
  • With continued stimulus from the Chinese government, domestic e-commerce is well positioned for a strong growth year.
  • Alibaba's AI models I believe will be more powerful than expected, which would explain why revenues in this division continue to increase in the triple digits. The West is realizing that China has not slept in these years, and Alibaba is the Asian pioneer in developing AI models. In fact, Apple has also chosen it.

I'm sure currently there is still a lot of pessimism toward China and some may still consider it “uninvestable,” so there is still massive capital available to support this trend. When that capital also pours into China, at that point it will be too late to buy Alibaba. I don't think we are at that stage yet, after all, the P/E Ratio FWD is still in the 16x range, and for such a company I expect it to reach at least 20x. However, consider that Chinese government stimulus is only now taking effect, and so I would not be surprised if Alibaba's EPS in 2025 is revised upward.

Based on current FWD EPS ($8.64), with a P/E Ratio of 20x, the fair value is $172 per share. But this represents a rather conservative scenario, and one that does not consider the strong enthusiasm pouring into China over the past month. I remind you that Alibaba was trading at $310 per share in 2020, and the demand for AI products was certainly not the same as it is today. All in all, I think Alibaba is a better company overall than it was 5 years ago, yet its price per share is not even half that. I think it is evident that there is still room for strong upside and I remain optimistic about the coming quarters, so Alibaba remains a strong buy. Of course, if the stock continues to appreciate at this rate, it will not be long before there is my first downgrade.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ASML Holding: Regression Analysis Shows 35% Upside Potential By 2027
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am bullish on ASML Holding N.V. (NASDAQ:ASML) due to its market dominance and strong tailwinds. I rate this stock a buy due to its double upside potential in a three-year time horizon based on my regression analysis. In this digital era, semiconductors play a critical role and at the center of this industry lies ASML which is a key player through its unique and valuable extreme ultraviolet [EUV] lithography systems. Its ultimate competitive MOAT lies here with no close competitor replicating its products and technology.

Its robust financial health doesn’t go unnoticed, which adds to its attractive attributes warranting a buy decision. For instance, the company has a 3-year revenue and EPS CAGR of 14.94% and 10.29% respectively. While this gives a glimpse of its strong financial performance, allow me to reflect on its performance over the last few years. First off, its revenue has seen an overall upward trajectory over the last 10 years, which in view reflects a sustainable strong demand for its products. However, it has exhibited instances of slowed growth which, I believe, is an indication of the cyclical nature of the semiconductor industry. This has not been different from its bottom lines, whereby the EBITDA and net income have demonstrated a positive overall slope but with instances of slowed growth, this can be explained majorly by the cyclical industry as well as varying expenses due to macro factors such as inflations.

Notably, despite the industry related challenge, the company’s profitability is nothing short of impressive. It has maintained double-digit margins despite economic challenges such as the inflationary 2022 and 2023 FYs. In my view, its strong profitability is a reflection of the company’s pricing power which originates from its market dominance.

The Competitive Landscape: ASML Dominance Is The Ultimate MOAT

I don’t think there is a better way to begin this section than by reminding you of this company’s niche which is the lithography technology which is very crucial in producing the most advanced semiconductor chips. With this in mind, it is important to mention that the company offers two main technologies which are the EUV and DUV lithography systems and this forms the basis of my market assessment.

I am acutely aware that most people argue that ASML is a monopoly, but to what extent do I agree or disagree with them? I will do this by focusing on the two market segments of its technologies. It is estimated that the company controls more than 90% of the global DUV lithography market, which clearly stipulates that it is not a pure monopoly on this front. However, with such a significant market command, this company is nearing full monopoly. One would wonder what makes this company command such a significant market share. In my view, the main reason is its advanced technology compared to its competitors Nikon and Canon. The main distinctive feature is its advanced immersion lithography systems such as the TWINSCAN NXT:2000i and TWINSCAN NXT:2100i, which enhances resolution and are more precise by submerging the wafer in a liquid medium. Such a technology is not applicable to Nikon which offers dry ArF and KrF scanners such as the NSR-S322F and Canon which offers nanoimprint lithography, and dry DUV like MPAsp. Below are a few advantages immersed DUV has over dry ones and which, I think, make ASML stand out and hence sustain its market position.

Let’s move to the other market segment which is the EUV lithography. In this area, ASML is the sole supplier of EUV lithography machines with a 100% market share and hence a monopoly. This implies that ASML is running a solo race in this lucrative market segment (EUV lithography which is critical in the most advanced chips). What a competitive advantage!

Another key factor of this company’s competitive position is its partnership with leading chipmakers with TSM, Samsung, and Intel being the top three customers accounting for ~ 80% of its total revenue.

Such partnerships not only ensure a reliable revenue stream but also position this company at the forefront of lithography innovations.

From this background, it is clearly evident that ASML is a dominant force in this market, to me, the most important thing here is, does it matter? I believe it does for two main reasons;

  • Barriers to entry: ASML invests approximately 15% of its total revenue in R&D which in my view sustains its market position through continuous innovation. For example, in the recently concluded FY (2024) the company spent $4.46 Billion, which was 15.23% of its total revenue. If you compare this to Canon’s $2.14 billion, that is a gap of about $2.32 billion which not only prevents these existing competitors from matching its technological advancement but also prevents new entrants.
  • Technical barriers: Besides the technical knowledge required to replicate ASML’s unique technology, the technical barrier is even made complex to navigate through its 33311 patents globally which prevents competitors from duplicating its technology.

In a nutshell, this company’s market dominance serves as its MOAT and through it, it has established both technical barriers and barriers to entry. For this reason, I expect it to maintain its dominance which serves as the primary reason for its financial resilience.

Trade Wars: Is The MOAT Under Threat?

One of the hottest topics around the globe is the trade wars especially following President Trump’s policy proposals, and ASML is not an exclusion from it. Of the greatest concerns is the US-China situation which stretches beyond the two nations as far as ASML is concerned. Before getting into details, let’s reflect on the company's performance for 2023 and 2024 and see how critical the Chinese market is.

From these results, we can see that China accounts for a significant portion of the company’s net system sales, rising from 29% in 2023 to 41% in 2024. Now imagine the ongoing geopolitical climate between the US and China which has culminated in tariff wars. I am viewing this rivalry from the perspective of when the US pressured the Netherlands to restrict ASML’s sale of its EUVs since 2019. Given the ongoing chips war, I am concerned that the US-China rift could grow wide forcing further sanctions something which could see the existing situation potentially extend to DUV systems. While this possibility remains speculative, it is something we cannot rule out, with ASML expecting more pressure from the US for more restrictions on China.

This being one of its main markets, more sanctions could lead to lower revenue which could be mitigated by focusing on its other core markets such as the US and Taiwan, however, my concern here is the possibility of China accelerating its push for domestic alternative which could see ASML lose its market share in the long run.

So, what do I see here in a nutshell? From where I sit, I don’t think the Netherlands government could sanction China further, because by doing so they would lose a significant market. Again, even if this happened, I don’t think that given the heavy R&D needed and the technical barriers, China would reach EUV and DUV parity with ASML before 2030. Consequently, this company’s MOAT remains intact at least in the short and midterm. Either way, the intensity of the negative impact is mitigated through the Western subsidies which, I believe, will accelerate demand for EUV as I will discuss shortly.

Tailwinds: Why I Am Optimistic About Future Growth

I see several growth catalysts for this company, the first one being the AI boom which, I believe, will fuel EUV adoption. Among the key features of complex GPUs and CPUs is the exponential use of transistors to cope with the computational needs. This suits ASML’s technology where the company is the sole supplier of EUV lithography machines. With the AI chip market projected to grow by a CAGR of 29.4% by 2032, this would be a major growth lever for this company. To support how this growing market alludes to a relentless demand for ASML, at the end of 2024, its order backlog was estimated at about €36 billion. While this speaks about its sustainable and growing demand, it also reflects on its revenue visibility and stability.

Another key lever is its High-NA EUV which could be the next growth frontier. The company announced that its High-NA TWINSCAN EXE will retail at a whopping $380 million each, more than 2x as much as its existing Low-NA EUV lithography version. In my view, this speaks about the company’s pricing power. With the first orders for shipment expected this year, volume production in 2026, and having its key customers placing orders, I expect this to serve as a major boost to revenue and profitability, especially when mass production and shipment are done. Given the company’s market position, solid growth in the AI chip market, and its pricing power, I believe this new technology positions it in a better position to achieve its financial goals of €44-60 billion in revenue and a gross margin of 56-60% by 2030. Above all, with a total debt of $3.81 billion and cash of $13.20 billion, its net cash stands at $9.39 billion, signifying its strong balance sheet, specifically liquidity which can support future innovations, enabling the company to stay ahead of the competition.

Lastly, I see Western policies like the US Chips Act ($52 billion in grants by 2030 and tax incentives) and the EU Chips Act (€43 billion in public funding) as a key growth catalyst. Both of these acts prioritize autonomy by encouraging domestic production which, I believe, will benefit ASML order books. The tax incentives and grants to ASML customers such as Intel will boost their production capacities and abilities, which will see ASML orders grow because it's the primary source of advanced DUVs and the sole distributor of EUVs.

Mind you, the race has narrowed to the AI factor where the most advanced chips gives precedence to the most recent AI developments. This in turn brings us to the fact that EUV lithography is the key ingredient in this recipe and I remind you ASML is the sole supplier of this technology. Consequently, as each nation prefers to produce its own chips, the demand for EUV lithography is likely to grow benefiting ASML directly.

Let’s Model This: Regression Analysis (A 3-Year Price Target)

From my previous sections, I can summarize the analysis into two broader categories of variables, which are the micro and macro factors. Having said that, under my micro factors, I will have two major variables in the model which are revenue and EPS. Under the macro factors, I have given attention to market position and this justifies why market size will be one of the macro factors. The last variable that falls under macro factors is interest rate which will serve as my risk parameter in the model because of its negative impacts on consumers and companies especially borrowing costs.

Given this background, I would begin by answering the most likely question, why regression? The main reason here is its comprehensive scope where it is possible to model both micro and macro factors factoring in the impact of policy shifts. Above all, the model offers a clear observation of each variable's statistical significance, hence a reliable output which doesn’t depend on random chances. With this in mind, I ran my model at a 95% confidence interval for data stemming from the last 10 years. This brings me to another potential question; why a sample size of 10 years? Well, this has been informed by two major reasons. The first one is data availability. Ten years is the maximum number I could get the financial data for ASML from reliable sources like SA. The other main reason is relevance; this is from the policy and regulation perspective as well as current trends. A 10-year time frame captures the impact of most recent regulation developments and industry trends which would be relevant in modern-day decision-making.

In light of the above justification(s), below is my model output.

From these results, the model has an adjusted R square of 0.84%, meaning this model is a good fit because it accounts for 84% of the variation, which is way above the average (50%). In addition, all t-stats are more than 2 for positively correlated variables and more than -2 for positively correlated variables with all P values below 0.05 which is the confidence interval. This means that all the variables were of statistical significance and hence suitable predictors of the Y variable (Price).

The overall model is as follows;

Given the above general model, I will estimate my price target for 2027(3 years price target) by plugging the estimated X variables values by that year. For revenue and EPS, I will adopt estimates from Seeking Alpha which are $42.33 billion and $36.29 respectively. For the market size, I estimated it at $724.10 billion using the average growth for the last 10 years from the semiconductor market revenue data. I used an estimated interest rate of 3.36% according to Statista. Given these inputs, I arrived at a price target of about $1,008.54 which alludes to an upside potential of approximately 35% by 2027. Given this double-digit upside potential, I believe ASML is a buy.

Before I conclude this section, you should note that the price target is greatly reliant on the future estimates of the variables, and therefore in case of any significant changes in the values for whatever reason, my price target would be affected, and it is in such moments that follow-up coverage will be provided.

Risks

Although I am bullish on this stock, investors should be aware of its concentration risk with all its revenue accruing from the semiconductor industry. Even worse, its top three customers account for about 80% of its total revenue, which shows a lack of diversity and susceptibility in case a key customer shifts or is out of business. Further, the lack of diversity in other markets outside semiconductors leaves this company vulnerable to industry related shocks which could affect its performance adversely in case of an industry-specific downturn.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I reiterate my buy rating for ASML given its double-digit upside potential backed by its strong market position and growth catalysts.

data.title: Walmart Q4 Earnings: Continues To Top Expectations, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Shares in retail champion, Walmart (NYSE:WMT), strode into its Q4 earnings release with a significant tailwind at its back. Expectations of continued success, however, were high.

Ahead of the release, shares were hovering near new 52-week highs at an over 40x forward trading multiple. When I last covered WMT, shares had just reached a new all-time high after easily topping Q2 expectations. In my coverage, I noted that shares were fairly valued due in part to the risks associated with the lofty expectations set forth in its earnings guidance.

The stock has since risen another 45% to its current 52-week high trading range. Current earnings performance showed that WMT continues to demonstrate sales and earnings competency and is still capturing share from key retail demographics. While the success story can’t be refuted, I remain hesitant to add or initiate positioning at trading levels that continue to track well above expected averages. As such, I continue to view shares as a hold despite positive earnings performance.

WMT Stock Key Metrics

While market sentiment on WMT shares has clearly been bullish, with the stock trading at the top end of its 52-week trading range, the broader Seeking Alpha (“SA”) analyst community is more neutral. Some, in fact, are overly pessimistic. In the last 90 days, about 40% of those covering the stock have rated shares as either a “hold” or a “sell”, with a 1/3rd of those stating the latter.

The neutral sentiment is echoed by SA’s quant scores. Though WMT grades strongly on metrics such as profitability and its trading momentum, the nose-bleed valuation earns it a mark of “F”, as measured by SA.

Wall Street, on the other hand, is more bullish, with more than 90% of those covering the stock rating shares as either a “buy” or a “strong buy.” Though this group is bullish, many see limited upside potential remaining at just under 3%.

What Did WMT Stock Expect Heading Into Earnings Release?

Along with blowout results in Q3, WMT’s management team released another set of positive revisions to their full-year guidance.

For the full-year, WMT was expecting to see sales growth of between 4.8% and 5.1%, notably above the prior guidance of 3.75% and 4.75%. More to the bottom line, operating income growth was seen at a range of 8.5% to 9.25%.

The new guidance range figured for Q4 constant currency sales growth of between 3% and 4% and operating income growth of between 5% and 7.5%.

Altogether, the two were expected to contribute to a new adjusted EPS midpoint of $2.45/share. This was a nearly 4% increase from the previous midpoint.

WMT Results Recap

Walmart has remained a standout performer in the retail sector at a time when retail sales are slowing. Today's results were indicative of the continuing trend.

During the quarter, WMT reported comparable sales growth of 4.6%, including 16% growth in global e-commerce. The growing trend in sales stands in contrast with recent data on overall retail, which showed total retail sales declining more than expected.

The larger than expected decline in overall retail sales follows a holiday season that reflected modest holiday spending growth of 3.8%, according to the National Retail Federation ("NRF").

WMT's sales trend for the quarter outpaced broader retail averages, and this is not surprising. The company has benefitted from growth in its online platform as well as from increasing market share from higher income consumers.

On the earnings front, WMT reported quarterly adjusted operating income of 9.4% due in part to growth in membership income and improved profitability in the e-commerce model. The operating income rate was also supported by a 53-basis point increase in gross margin rates.

WMT ended the year on a strong note, with total full-year revenues growing 5.1%, with an operating income rate of 9.7%. The strong year ultimately enabled WMT to return a sizable portion of its earnings to shareholders, primarily in the form of a 13% increase in its dividend payout.

The forward outlook, which includes expectations of lower profitability, disappointed investors, and this likely contributed to the selling pressures in the stock immediately following the morning release.

Is WMT Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

Despite the pullback, Walmart continues to trade at the top end of its 52-week trading range, and for good reason. As its Q4 results show, WMT continues to demonstrate sales and earnings competency, with comparable metrics growing ahead of expectations. WMT’s share gains among all income groups have also provided the retailer with a wide competitive moat. Results also continue to validate WMT’s capex-heavy approach to growth in both their storefront and e-commerce models.

Shares in WMT are up nearly 80% over the last year, including another 15% run to start the year. In prior coverage, when the stock had just reached a then all-time high, I noted that shares were valued appropriately. At that time, the stock commanded a forward trading multiple of about 30x. Since then, the stock has risen another 40% and is currently sitting at an over 40x forward.

Sentiment was negative despite stronger-than-expected results, and this could potentially signal a pivot to a more measured view on the stock’s trading multiple. Shares in Costco (COST) fetch nearly 60x, and it wouldn’t be out of reason to assert that WMT could reach similar valuations. However, I would be hesitant to initiate new or added positioning in WMT at current trading valuations. The stock has continued to exceed expectations that are seemingly higher every quarter. At current valuations, WMT has little room for a miscue. Investors, in my view, would be better positioned entering the stock at a more discounted price point.

data.description:

Market Review

The MSCI World Index was down modestly in the fourth quarter of 2024, generating a return of -0.2%. Over the period, key themes included consistently strong corporate earnings reports, global central banks’ continued monetary easing policies, and the resulting implications of a busy election year that ushered in sweeping political changes around the world. The different economic outlooks in the U.S., which remained strong, and in Europe and Asia, which remained more challenged, also impacted investor sentiment. This divide manifested in regionalized equity market performance with U.S. stocks delivering gains while European and Asian stocks delivered negative returns in the quarter.

Reports from management teams throughout the earnings season relayed the familiar dynamics of a resilient but weakening consumer as well as elevated corporate spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure investment. Many consumer-facing companies reported slowdowns in spending, which were offset by promotional discounting and price cuts. While the market reacted positively to the announcement of a more pronounced stimulus strategy by the Chinese government during the quarter, a commensurate uplift in Chinese consumer spending has yet to come and companies continued to report lackluster results from the region. Investors also remained focused on returns generated by high levels of AI infrastructure investment.

Major central banks around the world continue to ease monetary policy by cutting key interest rates. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the Federal Funds Rate by 25 basis points (bps) both in November and December. Market participants viewed these cuts as supportive of growth. Investor enthusiasm was somewhat dampened when the Fed’s updated forecast projected 50 bps of rate cuts in 2025, down from their previous forecast of 100 bps. A reduced easing cadence hinted at the Fed’s concern of reemergent inflation, and investors also remain focused on the potentially inflationary nature of President-elect Donald Trump’s proposed fiscal policies. The European Central Bank delivered two rate cuts, while the Bank of England issued a rate cut in its November meeting and then held rates steady in its December meeting. While rates broadly remain in restrictive territory, global central banks have generally adopted gradual, data-dependent approaches to easing.

In December, France’s new Prime Minister was replaced amid disagreement over the country’s budget and Germany called for snap elections amid concerns of an economic slowdown. Likewise, consternation arose throughout Asia after the U.S. election, as the likelihood of new tariffs and more protectionist policies from America may hurt Asia’s export dependent economies.

The Fund trailed the Index in the quarter, as a negative selection effect outweighed a positive allocation effect. The Fund’s relative performance was supported by its holdings in the financials and communication sectors, which resulted in positive selection effects. data.title: RPAR Risk Parity ETF: The Path To 8% Annual Returns Or More
data.description:
There is no sugar-coating it: my track record on multi-asset class and diversified investing has been dismal in the past five years. I first issued a "strong buy" rating on the RPAR Risk Parity ETF (NYSEARCA:RPAR) in July 2020. Since then, distributions included, the fund has remained flat (i.e., no meaningful gains or losses) while the S&P 500 (SPY) doubled in value, as depicted below.

It turns out that betting on a stock-and-bond combo, the "meat and potatoes" of the risk-parity ETF, since 2021 has yielded among the worst three-year returns in history. While I cannot guarantee that things will improve substantially in the immediate future, I have reasons to believe that RPAR could deliver high single-digit, if not low double-digit returns per year over the next decade at least. RPAR and its close cousin UPAR Ultra Risk Parity ETF (UPAR) remain a strong buy, in my view.

A quick word on RPAR

For the benefit of those unfamiliar with risk parity, the strategy aims to combine assets that have low correlations in an attempt to capture the free-lunch effect of diversification in an efficient manner. Efficient, in this case, means investing less in high-volatility assets, and more in low-volatility ones. Doing so, theoretically, better balances the portfolio by not overly exposing it to the riskiest holdings (usually stocks).

Since allocating more capital to less volatile asset classes naturally results in lower expected returns, RPAR makes up for it by leveraging its total exposure to the underlying assets at a factor of around 1.2 times (for UPAR, the leverage factor is 1.7 times, while all else is very much the same). Below is a visual representation of RPAR's target asset and risk allocations.

RPAR: Bad news (past), good news (future)

On paper, being balanced across domestic and international stocks, bonds, precious metals, and commodity producers sounds like a reasonable, relatively safe way to invest. This has been the case throughout the past century.

For simplicity, think of a portfolio that invests 70% in ten-year treasuries; 40% in the S&P 500; and 10% in gold (notice the 1.2x leverage at play here). This allocation would roughly emulate RPAR's target asset allocation depicted above. In the 90 years between 1932 and 2021, such a portfolio would have returned a respectable 9.1% per year, with the worst year at -13.2% in 1937 being substantially better than the S&P 500's -36.6% in 2008.

But in 2022, the strategy would have delivered a whopping -19.6%, the second-worst performance since the 1920s. On a three-year rolling basis, the negative returns seen between 2021 and 2023 would have only been better than the three-year periods that ended in 1931, 1932, and 1941 (see chart below).

The above is a reminder of how unusual it is for a diversified asset class strategy to perform so poorly as it has recently -- driven, primarily, by a massive and still lasting bear market in bonds following the post-COVID spike in inflation and interest rates. It is also a subtle argument for why things should improve from here. Judging by the historical data alone, I would expect future returns in RPAR to improve substantially and more closely match the 9% or so annualized returns seen over the long run.

My beliefs are further supported by CAPM (the capital asset pricing model). With the risk-free rate today hovering around 4.5%, and assuming a market risk premium of 5%, I would expect the S&P 500 to return around 9.5% per year going forward, give or take a couple of percentage points and the expected month-to-month ebbs and flows in stock prices.

In addition, as I have presented in the past on Seeking Alpha, returns in treasuries and treasury funds over the life of the underlying security (i.e., ten years in the case of ten-year treasuries) approximate their yield. That is: investing in intermediate-term US government bonds today should return approximately 4.5% in the next one or two decades.

If the above proves to be true, a 1.2x leveraged strategy could return 7% per year over the longer term, possibly 8% if gold prices climb by about 10% per year from here. This already respectable rate of return could be higher, and possibly reach double-digit territory, if bond yields finally begin to moderate from their current levels, as they were expected to until recently.

Final remarks on RPAR

Of course, investing in risky assets (stocks, bonds, commodities, and anything that is not cash) comes with uncertainty. It is impossible to categorically affirm that leveraged risk parity will finally produce the moderate-to-high returns that are expected of it, especially in the shorter term.

But relative to single-asset bets (e.g., all-in on the S&P 500), I think that owning RPAR today makes a lot of sense from a risk-reward perspective -- even though I have been burned by this ETF and by diversified asset class investing at large in the past few years.

data.title: Graham Corporation: Slightly Weak Results But Overreaction Creates A Buying Opportunity.
data.description:
Introduction

I initiated a buy rating on Graham Corporation (NYSE:GHM) last month and since then, the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 index by falling ~9% as compared to the S&P 500 increasing ~6% at the same time. Although the stock was trending up post my initial coverage, it tanked post the earnings announcement on February 7, 2025.

On the surface, GHM has reported mixed numbers; however, the fall in order intake made the markets uneasy. I believe the lower order intake is a part and parcel of GHM’s business, as much of its orders are lumpy in nature and one should not conclude slow demand from low order intake from just a single quarter. Nevertheless, the stock price has tanked due to this concern, which has widened the return expectation and margin of safety on the stock. Hence, I have retained my initial buy rating on the GHM.

3Q25 recap

GHM’s 3Q25 result give or take was somewhat better than expected on the bottom-line front, but the topline was a slight miss. The company reported topline growth of ~7.3% YoY as compared to the consensus estimate of ~13% growth. On the bottom-line front, the company beat the consensus non-GAAP EPS estimate by $0.11 and reported $0.18 of non-GAAP EPS.

On the surface, the topline miss looks a bit disappointing, but it is just normal for a business like GHM. GHM's sales cycle includes booking an order, then working on it and then transferring these orders to its customers, resulting in revenue. Mostly these orders take a couple of quarters to materialize in sales. Although there are mechanisms like over-time recognition and the percentage of completion method to recognize sales regularly, there are times when the timing of revenue recognition tends to make the growth figure a bit lumpy, which makes it an unreliable indicator for the underlying growth of the company. In this quarter’s context, the project timing issue relating to the refining segment led to the slower topline growth. In addition, the third quarter is typically the slowest quarter among all the quarters due to the holiday season being in the quarter, resulting in further slowness in topline growth.

As mentioned earlier, sales growth does not reflect the true underlying picture of GHM’s business; order growth tends to give a good leading picture of how the company is performing. For the 3Q25, the order growth was not that encouraging as well, dropping significantly from the 3Q24 order intake of $123 million to a meager $24.8 million level. The primary reason for such a large drop was due to a significantly tough comparison of prior year’s quarter, which was fueled by large follow-on orders by the US Navy in 3QFY24. Moreover, one could also see how lumpy the order flows had been for GHM in the last 3 years.

I believe viewing GHM’s future prospect with a dip in order intake for a single quarter should be unjust for the company. However, the overall trend for the last few quarters should be factored in the analysis, which is still positive. While the order intake was a weak spot for the quarter, its YTD book-to-bill ratio is still near 1x. Since the company plans to grow 8-10% organically over the long term, I believe the book-to-bill ratio should trend toward 1.1x in the coming quarters.

On the profitability front, GHM reported a 260 bps improvement in gross margins to 24.8%. The gross margins are now starting to trend near the 25%+ long-term gross margins guidance. While gross margins improved 260 bps, its leverage was not visible in adjusted EBITDA margins, which improved by just 180 bps. This was due to higher SG&A expenses due to investment in people, processes, and technologies and implementation of a new ERP system in the Batavia facility. This adjusted EBITDA number also includes higher employee expenses to the Barber Nichols business unit, which totaled to ~$1.1 million, or 230 bps of total revenue. This extra compensation should wear off starting from 2026 and adjusted EBITDA margins should start to trail near low teens post-wearing off of this expense item.

Overall, the results were a little off on the order growth front, which, I believe, is just a timing and lumpiness of orders issue. Margin expansion is on target and provides a runway for operational leverage going forward. With respect to guidance, the management has maintained all the guidance it has given earlier, except guidance for gross margins and SG&A. Management has guided for a 100 bps better gross margins now than earlier and a 100 bps higher SG&A cost now than earlier. The net impact on the adjusted EBITDA margins should remain negligible.

Valuation

I do not feel anything much has changed on the fundamental basis from my last coverage on GHM but the price has corrected a little. Since fundamentals are the same, I would use similar assumptions to project EBITDA as used earlier.

Assumptions

Revenue: 8-10% revenue growth as guided by management, not including growth from acquisitions, which is highly likely due to a good balance sheet and management’s intent to grow inorganically.

EBITDA margins: Assumed in line with management guidance and a little lower for FY27 (management guidance is for low to mid teens).

EV/EBITDA multiple: Multiple has been slightly modified as the peer comparison multiples have been changed.

Net Debt: Assumed to be constant over the years even though GHM would generate cashflow going forward.

After considering these assumptions, I have arrived at a price target of ~$60 per share for FY27, which is 55% higher than the current market price of its stock.

Risk

GHM has reported a slower order intake for 3Q25, although the order intake at times is lumpy but continued slower order intake in subsequent quarters should be seen as a change in business dynamics. Moreover, the actions taken by the Department of Government Efficiency [DOGE] should also be monitored carefully, as DOGE is aimed at cutting costs in US shipbuilding.

Conclusion

The recent result was neither that positive nor is it a clear negative for the company. According to my understanding, the fundamentals are still intact for the company and the slowness in order intake is just the nature of GHM’s business. However, the market has taken it as a clear negative, knocking the stock price more than it deserves. This fall has increased my margin of safety and return expectation from the stock. Due to this, I am retaining the buy rating on the GHM.

Market Review

Equity markets were mixed in the final quarter of 2024 as the re-election of Donald J. Trump and rate cuts buoyed US equities, while tariff threats and slowing economic growth pressured other markets. For the quarter, the S&P 500® Index added 2.41%, while the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of non-US developed markets, declined - 8.11% and the MSCI Emerging Markets Index declined -8.01%. Artificial intelligence (AI) once again drove growth stocks to handily outperform their value peers while large-cap stocks bested their small-cap counterparts.

Quarterly Performance

In the US, market advances were fueled by rate cuts in November and December, as well as hopes of pro-business policies and promises of tax cuts by President-elect Trump. However, gains were tempered in December after the US Federal Reserve (Fed) signaled the potential for fewer rate cuts in 2025. Market breadth again narrowed during the quarter with over half of the sectors in the S&P 500 posting losses. In addition to AI-fueled gains in the communication services, information technology and consumer discretionary sectors, financial stocks advanced on the hopes of a more regulatory-friendly environment under the new presidential administration. Conversely, the materials sector continued to be hampered by slowing growth in China, while health-care stocks faced multiple headwinds following the election.

Economic growth remained solid, buoyed by resilient consumer spending. The 10-year US Treasury yield ended the quarter higher, and the US dollar surged on election results and President-elect Trump’s pro-US economic agenda.

Developed international markets fell as European stocks plummeted around interest-rate uncertainty and fears of tariffs under the new US presidential administration. With inflation moving closer to target, the European Central Bank reduced interest rates twice. However, economic growth remained sluggish, political strife continued in France and the German government collapsed. In Germany, the region’s largest economy, both consumer and business sentiment suffered and the risk of recession rose over the quarter. In the UK, the Bank of England cut rates in November but then held them steady in December given growing inflation forecasts. Core consumer inflation in Japan remained above the central bank's 2% target; however, the Bank of Japan left rates unchanged.

Emerging market equities retreated for the quarter, facing pressure from a sluggish Chinese economy, a strengthening US dollar and concerns over potential tariffs. While Chinese equities rallied in December, they ended the quarter lower on concerns over economic growth. Authorities have stepped up measures to support the economy, including rate cuts, relaxed home-buying restrictions, purchases of sovereign bonds and multi-billion injections into outright reverse repurchase agreements. India’s economy slowed in the third quarter due to weaker expansion in manufacturing and decreasing consumption. In Brazil, stocks underperformed amid worries over the government's commitment to cut public spending and reign in the growing budget deficit.

Commodities advanced for the quarter, bolstered by natural-gas prices. An early and frigid start of winter in North America and Europe, in addition to the end of Russian supplies to Europe, pushed prices higher. Weak demand from China and surplus concerns continued to pressure crude oil. While starting the quarter higher, gold prices lagged due to the strengthening US dollar, the US election results and the dissipation of tensions in the Middle East. The Dow Jones Commodity Index ended the quarter up by 0.49%.

Performance Review

The fund (Class A shares at NAV) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, during the fourth quarter of 2024. From a sector perspective, consumer discretionary led relative contributors over the period, followed by communication services. Conversely, industrials and consumer staples were the most significant laggards.

Sector Review

Positive Impacts

Consumer Discretionary: Consumer discretionary led relative contributors over the period, largely driven by stock selection among the hotels, restaurants & leisure space. In particular, Dutch Bros Class A (BROS) was a top contributor after reporting third quarter results, which exceeded expectations, and raised full year guidance.

Communication Services: Stock selection among communication services also contributed to relative returns, particularly among the interactive media & services segment. Our holding of Reddit Class A (RDDT) buoyed returns after a solid quarter as machine translation continues to be a daily active user driver. Additionally, being in the public market has helped Reddit’s notoriety with potential new users as well. The market continues to be positive on the company’s ability to pull engagement levers and improve its internal search capabilities.

Negative Impacts

Industrials: Industrials led relative detractors by sector over the period. Stock selection among the trading companies & distributors segment and the building products segment weighed on results. In particular, our holding of Builders FirstSource (BLDR) was a top detractor as fundamentals remain challenged in the housing market, but we expect this to change next year as the Fed cuts rates and the industry recovers.

Consumer Staples: Consumer staples also detracted from relative returns over the period, primarily due to an overweight to the beverages space.

Stock Review1

Key Contributors – Q4 24

Reddit Class A: Reddit operates as an online discussion platform. The company publicizes online items recommended by members and aggregates news stories submitted by users. Shares of Reddit Class A had a solid quarter as machine translation continues to be a daily active user driver. Additionally, being in the public market has helped Reddit’s notoriety with potential new users as well. The market continues to be positive on the company’s ability to pull engagement levers and improve its internal search capabilities.

Axon Enterprise: Axon Enterprise integrates a suite of hardware devices and cloud software solutions. The company's three product categories include TASER, sensors and software. Axon performed well as the company continues to deliver a strong platform of hardware and software solutions to global public safety end markets. Axon is at the forefront of enabling and capturing the digitalization of evidence, an important secular theme we have identified over the next decade.

Dutch Bros Class A: Dutch Bros operates and franchises drive-thru beverage shops in the United States. Shares of Dutch Bros rose over the period after reporting third quarter results, which exceeded expectations, and raised full year guidance.

Key Detractors – Q4 24

TransMedics Group (TMDX): TransMedics manufactures and sells an organ transplant system, allowing a donor organ to be monitored and infused in transport so it is more viable when it arrives at the implant hospital. Shares weighed on results over the quarter after the company reduced its fourth quarter and 2025 growth rate due to lower transplant volumes serviced.

Builders FirstSource: Builders FirstSource is a supplier and manufacturer of building materials, manufactured components and construction services to professional homebuilders, sub-contractors, remodelers and consumers. The company offers an integrated solution to its customers by providing manufacturing, supply and installation of a full range of structural and related building products. Shares fell as fundamentals remain challenged in the housing market, but we expect this to change next year as the Fed cuts rates and the industry potentially recovers.

Inspire Medical Systems: Inspire Medical Systems develops and markets a device that reduces sleep apnea by stimulating the hypoglossal nerve that connects with the tongue. This procedure is specifically for patients with obstructive sleep apnea derived from tongue obstruction, not lateral obstruction. Inspire underperformed in the fourth quarter owing to a re-rate that occurred in the stock in the third quarter. The market is bearish on near term company prospects owing to their 2025 launch of their next generation sleep apnea implant which may delay procedures in the fourth quarter of 2024 and the first quarter of 2025. Patients and doctors may want to wait for the new upgraded implant, thus reducing sales results for these two near-term quarters. As longer-term investors, we do not get mired in the short-term market concerns when the long-term outlook for tongue-based sleep apnea prevalence remains high and Inspire has, in our opinion, a strong product to treat it. Our estimates indicate that just 6% of the available patient pool has been penetrated.

Market Outlook

Predicting the outlook for the equity markets is challenging given a projected earnings growth of almost 13% in 2024 and the S&P trading at 21.6 times forward earnings, combined with expectations for more Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts despite inflation. While the Fed initiated rate cuts this past September, the bond market is signaling to investors that it does not buy the lower inflation story or weakening economy, as evidenced by 10-year yields that have only risen since then. Simultaneously, economic surprise indices have declined and are not contributing to higher yields. As the central bank grapples with balancing economic growth and inflation control, its actions may have far-reaching implications. In response to the evolving economic landscape, the Fed may opt for a range of policy measures. A continuation of the shift to accommodative monetary policies, such as lower interest rates, may be at risk given the resilience of the economy and inflation. If inflationary pressures persist, the Fed might change course and move to adopt a more hawkish stance by either holding current rates steady or even raising interest rates to temper economic overheating. This could lead to increased market volatility as investors adjust their expectations. Sustained higher interest rates would result in increased borrowing costs for businesses and consumers, potentially dampening economic growth and corporate profits.

Trump's 2.0 agenda blends pro-market and populist policies. We are optimistic about potential deregulation and tax cuts, while bond markets are wary of inflation and fiscal deficits. Success may depend on balancing trade, immigration and deficit policies, as well as resolving geopolitical conflicts. From an investment perspective, DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) represents a notable development from Washington, presenting an opportunity to address fiscal issues that could lead to higher risk-free interest rates irrespective of inflation levels.

Advancements in this area may not generate widespread enthusiasm in Washington, D.C., which typically resists change.

In our view, the probability of a recession in 2025 remains low due to robust corporate profits, strong labor markets and tight credit spreads. However, given that the S&P is currently trading at nearly 22 times forward earnings, the Chicago Board Options Exchange's Volatility Index (VIX) stands at around 17, and Bitcoin hovers around the $100,000 milestone, it is evident that expectations for risk assets remain elevated. With the largest 10 stocks comprising almost 39% of the S&P 500, much of the active large-cap managers' outperformance this year could hinge on the duration of the artificial intelligence (AI) trade's success. While we are optimistic about the continued success of the AI trade, we remain vigilant. Companies' ability to manage costs and maintain profit margins may be under scrutiny. One of the important highlights for 2025 may be the monetization of the broad AI efforts and spend over the last two years. Other factors such as supply-chain disruptions and labor-market tightness could exert upward pressure on costs, challenging businesses to find efficiencies and pass on price increases to consumers.

Geopolitical factors are set to play a significant role in shaping the equity outlook for 2025. Key areas of focus include tensions between major economies, regional conflicts and international trade policies. China's economic policies and performance is expected to have significant implications for global markets in 2025. The Chinese government could continue its focus on achieving stable growth while addressing structural challenges, such as debt levels and demographic shifts. Policies aimed at stimulating domestic consumption and reducing reliance on exports may support economic resilience. However, regulatory interventions in key sectors and geopolitical tensions could introduce uncertainty. We will be closely monitoring developments in China's property market, technological sector and trade relations with key partners, including the US. The relationship between the US and China could be particularly influential, with ongoing trade negotiations and strategic competition impacting market dynamics. Any progress toward de-escalation or cooperation could provide a boost to global equities, while heightened tensions could lead to market volatility. In Europe, the political landscape should be closely watched as countries navigate post-Brexit adjustments and address economic challenges, such as energy security and fiscal stability. Emerging markets, too, could present both opportunities and risks, with investors weighing growth potential against political and economic stability.
